narration.default: In Scratch, the "change [score] by 1" block was a single drag — quick, easy. The long way in C would be: score = score + 1; — that works but it's ugly when you do it dozens of times. C gives you SHORTCUTS that mean exactly the same thing in fewer characters. The shortcuts are: += (add and store), -= (subtract and store), *= (multiply and store), /= (divide and store), ++ (add 1), -- (subtract 1). Today you'll see all four of the most common ones used on a single score variable. Once you learn these, you'll see them in pretty much every C program you ever read.

Line 5: score += 5; — this is exactly the same as writing score = score + 5; just shorter. Read it as "increase score by 5" or "score plus-equals 5". After this line runs, score holds 15 (it was 10, plus 5). The same pattern works for any operation: -= subtracts, *= multiplies, /= divides. So count *= 2; doubles count, total /= 4; quarters total, etc. Use these shortcuts everywhere — they're standard C style and other programmers expect to see them.

Line 7: score -= 2; — same shape as +=, opposite direction. score was 15, subtract 2, now it's 13. In Scratch you'd use "change [lives] by -1" when the player lost a life — same idea. Useful for scoring penalties, countdowns, paying for items in a game shop, anywhere a number needs to drop.

Line 9: score++; — adds exactly 1 to score. This shortcut is so famous that the language C++ is named after it (C++ is "C, with one added"). Why have a special shortcut just for +1? Because adding 1 happens all the time in loops — every time around a loop, you usually bump a counter by 1. score++ is shorter than score += 1, which is shorter than score = score + 1. They all do the same thing. After this line, score is 14. There is also score++ vs ++score (with the ++ in front) — they have a subtle difference, but for now think of them as the same. We'll dig in later if needed.

Line 11: score--; — subtracts exactly 1 from score. Same shape as ++, opposite direction. After this line, score is 13. Common use: lives--, time--, ammo-- in a game; or counting down in a loop ("for i from 10 down to 1, do something"). Together with ++, this pair (++ and --) is one of the most-typed pieces of code in all of C.

Trace it in your head before clicking: score starts at 10. After +=5 it's 15. After -=2 it's 13. After ++ it's 14. After -- it's 13 again. Click Run. The Console should show exactly that sequence: 15, 13, 14, 13. Each printf shows the value AT THAT MOMENT. These four shortcuts are everywhere in real C code — get comfortable with all of them.
